topic: Transformer architecture improvements in large language models 2020-2024
final_review:
The field of large language models has witnessed significant advancements in recent years, with a focus on improving the Transformer architecture (Gao et al., 2023). One area of research has been the development of more efficient sequence modeling techniques. Another approach has been the use of retrieval-augmented generation, which has been shown to improve the performance of large language models (Gao et al., 2023). 

In addition to these architectural improvements, there has been a growing interest in applying large language models to specific domains, such as chemistry (Bran et al., 2024) and medicine (Van Veen et al., 2024). For example, large language models have been used to predict chemical properties (Heid et al., 2023) and to generate trustworthy health supplement content (Holter et al., 2018). However, the use of large language models in these domains also raises concerns about explainability and transparency (Zhao et al., 2024). 

The use of large language models in autonomous agents has also been explored, with a survey highlighting the potential applications and challenges of this approach (Wang et al., 2024). Furthermore, the integration of large language models with other AI technologies, such as computer vision, has been shown to be effective in applications such as medical image segmentation (Wang et al., 2022). 

Despite these advancements, there are still significant research gaps in the field, including the need for more efficient and scalable architectures, as well as the need for more robust evaluation metrics (Zhao et al., 2024). Additionally, the use of large language models in real-world applications raises concerns about trustworthiness and reliability (Yang et al., 2024). 

In terms of agreements and contradictions, there is a general consensus that large language models have the potential to revolutionize a wide range of applications (Bran et al., 2024; Van Veen et al., 2024). However, there are also concerns about the limitations and challenges of these models, including their lack of explainability and transparency (Zhao et al., 2024). 

In conclusion, the field of large language models is rapidly evolving, with significant advancements in architectural improvements, domain-specific applications, and integration with other AI technologies. However, there are still significant research gaps and challenges that need to be addressed, including the need for more efficient and scalable architectures, more robust evaluation metrics, and more robust and trustworthy models.

References:
Bran, A. M., Cox, S., & Schilter, O. (2024). Augmenting large language models with chemistry tools. https://doi.org/10.1038/s42256-024-00832-8
Gao, Y., Xiong, Y., & Gao, X. (2023). Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2312.10997
Heid, E., Greenman, K. P., & Chung, Y. (2023). Chemprop: A Machine Learning Package for Chemical Property Prediction. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.jcim.3c01250
Holter, O. M., & Ell, B. (2018). AI-Assisted Pipeline for Dynamic Generation of Trustworthy Health Supplement Content at Scale. https://doi.org/10.4230/lipics.cosit.2022.18
Van Veen, D., Van Uden, C., & Blankemeier, L. (2024). Adapted large language models can outperform medical experts in clinical text summarization. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-024-02855-5
Wang, L., Ma, C., & Feng, X. (2024). A survey on large language model based autonomous agents. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11704-024-40231-1
Wang, R., Lei, T., & Cui, R. (2022). Medical image segmentation using deep learning: A survey. https://doi.org/10.1049/ipr2.12419
Yang, J., Jin, H., & Tang, R. (2024). Harnessing the Power of LLMs in Practice: A Survey on ChatGPT and Beyond. https://doi.org/10.1145/3649506
Zhao, H., Chen, H., & Yang, F. (2024). Explainability for Large Language Models: A Survey. https://doi.org/10.1145/3639372
